title: 🚀 GEO Agent has huge potential, but it needs stronger SEO features and tracking
content:
Hi Climbo team,

I have an important question about the GEO Agent.

I like the feature and I see a lot of potential, but if we really want to scale with it, I think the GEO Agent needs to become more than just an automatic blog generator.

Right now, the blogs are mainly based on the Google Business Profile and reviews.
That is a good start, but for strong SEO blogs, it is still too limited.

Especially because the additional input is currently limited to around 500 characters.

That makes it difficult to properly explain the business, services, target audience, important locations, keywords, competitors, tone of voice and the full SEO strategy.

When I look at tools like WP SEO AI, I see that businesses are willing to pay a lot for AI SEO blogs, mainly because they are positioned as a real SEO growth engine.

That is why I would love to know what updates are planned for the GEO Agent.
For me, the most important improvements would be:
[ol:1][li]A larger SEO prompt per client
Not 500 characters, but a full prompt where we can properly explain the business, target audience, services, locations, tone of voice, keywords, competitors and strategy.
[ol:2][li]Website scraping
The GEO Agent should be able to read the client’s website, so the blogs match the actual services, pages, language and positioning of the business.
[ol:3][li]SEO keywords and search intent
Instead of choosing random topics, the agent should create blogs based on keywords, search intent and real opportunities people are searching for.
[ol:4][li]Competitor analysis
The agent could look at what competitors are writing about and identify content gaps or opportunities to rank better.
[ol:5][li]Blog performance tracking
For example, being able to see how many times a blog page has been read, which blogs are getting traffic, which topics perform well and which blogs are not doing much.
[ol:6][li]SEO score per blog
A simple score or checklist per blog, based on title, meta description, headings, keyword usage, internal links and readability.

Watch the AMA recording to see the full walkthrough, and let us know your feedback.

title: Thoughts on the Gating Update: Protecting our Agency Future in 2026
content:
Hey [@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) and everyone,

First off, a huge thank you to Giacomo for acknowledging the review gating concerns on the call yesterday.
The transparency and the speed at which you guys move is exactly why I’m such a fan of Climbo, the new AI feature is truly a game changer for all agencies!

I wanted to share a few food-for-thought points regarding the short-term solution of moving the gating toggle to the agency level:

1. Google Policy is Global, not Local :

While we often talk about the US FTC rules \(which are now at $53k per violation\), Google’s Terms of Service don't change at the border. Their AI doesn't care if a business is in Rome, London, or New York , it’s looking for the same data fingerprint of filtered reviews. If the pattern is there, the penalty is the same: the "Badge of Shame" warning banner or a total profile shadow ban.

2. The Platform Signature Risk :

This is my biggest concern with leaving the toggle active at all. Google’s 2026 AI is hyper efficient at identifying "Review Steering" patterns. If even a small percentages of Climbo agencies use gating, Google’s algorithm can flag the entire domain or code-structure used by Climbo. This means your compliant clients could lose their rankings because other agencies on the same platform are using the toggle.This means even agencies that play by the rules could be guilty by association if Google decides the software itself facilitates deceptive practices.

3. Beyond the FTC:

The UK & EU rules: The FTC is the big name in the US, but for those of us with global clients or operating in those areas, the stakes are even higher.
The UK’s DMCCA and the EU’s Omnibus Directive now allow for fines up to 10% of global turnover.
As agency owners, if we provide a toggle that leads to a fine like that, we are the ones in the crosshairs for professional negligence. The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission \(ACCC\) has officially labeled "manipulative digital practices" as a top enforcement priority for the 2026-27 period. Which is even more aggressive and bigger fines than the FTC.

title: 🤩 Employee Tracking, GEO Agent, New UI & What’s Next – A.M.A. Recap (23/04/2026)
content:
Hi Climbers!

Here’s a quick recap of yesterday’s AMA and what we covered.

We started with some clarifications on the GEO Agent. We went deeper into how the system will generate localized, SEO-focused content using real business data and reviews, and how you can position this feature in your offer. This is just the first version, and we’ll keep refining it based on your feedback.

We also presented the new Client Mode UI, where all features are now grouped into three main areas: Reputation, Visibility, and Engagement. This is a UI improvement designed to make the platform much clearer and easier to use for local business owners, without changing any existing functionality.

A big part of the AMA was dedicated to the employee tracking & leaderboard feature, which is now ready and will be live today in a few hours.

With this feature, businesses can add their employees \(with name, photo, and aliases\) and generate a dedicated review link and QR code for each one. Every link tracks unique visits, allowing the business to see which employee is actively asking for reviews.

We’ve also added the ability to assign reviews to employees when their alias is mentioned inside the review text. This means employees are not only motivated to generate visits, but also to build real relationships with customers and encourage them to leave more personal, authentic reviews.

On top of that, we’re introducing a public leaderboard \(last 7 days, 30 days, all-time\) where businesses can rank employees based on performance. This creates a natural incentive system, where employees are encouraged to ask more customers for reviews.

The goal is to create a simple but powerful gamification system that businesses can use to reward top performers and increase review generation across the team.

Looking ahead, we shared what’s coming next on the Agency Mode side, including improvements like moving clients from free to paid plans more smoothly and introducing sales team management features.

Finally, we mentioned some ongoing updates, including work on Apple Business and Google Sheets integration.

title: Potential Issue with the FTC in the US
content:
Guys, I'm sure that you may have thought of this, or at least I hope you have.

The FTC ruling on "Review Gating" potentially impacts Climbo in the USA. The link to leave a "Public Review" needs to be more conspicuous. Meaning equal size to the button above it to leave private feedback.

It does comply with "Uniform Solicitation" guidelines but it "may not" comply with the "Two Path" system guidelines. The text is below:

The "Two-Path" System: You ask a customer about their experience first \(e.g., "How was your stay?"\). If they say "Great," you send them the "Public Review" link. If they say "Bad," you direct them to a private internal feedback form. This is direct review gating and is illegal.

By the way, this is directly from an attorney that is licensed to argue cases in Federal Court. So guys, you need to increase the size of the link on BOTH version 1.0 and 2.0 since you are probably exposing some of us in the US to EXISTING clients.

Please help!

title: Disable from Billing Section
content:
Just had a setup win wanted to make sure everyone knew this to prevent other tickets for the Climbo team. I am not using Stripe as I have an external subscription system that has this and other software together. Because I was not using the Stripe I thought I had to check the "Disable from billing section" in the plan settings. This is not the case. If you select this there will be no upgrade options in the billing section for the client. You will want to disable this option as seen in image one so that the upgrade options show in the user billing section as upgrade options \(second image\). If you check then only the clients current plan will show.

So the difference is if you are using Stripe they can immediately upgrade if you are not it says "Contact Us to Switch"

Having said that for all of us not using the Stripe integration it would be great to have an option to alter the text or preferably add our own upgrade link URL so the clients are not dead ended with a generic contact us with no action link leading to a manual hard to upgrade process with no direct contact or upgrade option as it sits. So if that is something you guys would like would appreciate you adding it as a feature request here: [https://roadmap.climbo.com/?t=requests](https://roadmap.climbo.com/?t=requests) or upvote once in the planned list so be sure to check there first. title: Custom Branded Review Posts for a Professional Social Media Presence
content:
Problem to Solve:
Currently, when public reviews are shared to Instagram, Facebook, and upcoming TikTok integrations, the templates look generic and one-size-fits-all. This can clash with a business’s brand identity, color palette, typography, and overall social media aesthetic.
For agencies and businesses focused on branding, visuals matter. Generic templates can make review-sharing feel disconnected from the rest of their profile.

Feature Idea:
Allow users/agencies to create or upload custom branded templates for public review sharing.
When a review is posted automatically or manually shared, users could choose a template that matches their business identity.

Example Use Cases:
Restaurant / Café
Use warm tones, elegant fonts, logo placement, and a premium food aesthetic.
Dental Clinic
Use clean white/blue branding with a professional minimal design.
Streetwear Brand
Use dark bold templates with modern typography matching their Instagram feed.

Suggested Features
[ul][li]Custom colors[li]Font selection[li]Logo upload[li]Background image or texture[li]Layout choices[li]Story / Post / Reel dimensions[li]Save multiple templates[li]Client-specific template presets[li]Auto-apply chosen template when sharing reviews

How It Would Benefit Workflow
[ul][li]Makes automated review posting feel premium instead of generic[li]Increases the likelihood that clients will actually use social posting tools[li]Better brand consistency across social platforms[li]Helps agencies justify higher pricing[li]More visually appealing profiles = stronger trust online[li]Saves time vs designing every post manually in Canva

Why This Matters?
Many businesses care about aesthetics, especially restaurants, beauty brands, gyms, clothing brands, salons, and modern service businesses. Their social feed is part of their reputation.
Reviews are powerful social proof—but only if presented in a way that fits the brand.

Examples from Other Tools
Canva, Later, Adobe Express, and social media schedulers emphasize brand kits and templates because businesses want consistent visuals.

title: A few important questions about the blog feature
content:
I have a few questions about the blogs, mainly because this is important when explaining it properly to clients.

1. How does the visibility actually work?If the blog is connected to a domain, how does that help with discoverability in practice?
For example:if a blog is written about food in The Hague, is the idea that when people search for something like food in The Hague, that blog could appear in search results?
I’m using that as a simple example, but I’d love to understand better how the visibility side works, because that part really matters when explaining the value to clients.

2. How do we actually place the blogs on the website?Let’s say clients use the website through our system and everything is linked with their Google Business Profile.
I currently don’t see an option for how those blogs get added to the website itself.So how does that part work exactly?

3. What happens when there aren’t enough reviews left?For example:if a business has 4 reviews, and 4 blogs have already been created, what happens with blog number 5?
Does it:
[ul][li]still write a blog, but without using a review[li]reuse an older review[li]or stop generating blogs entirely
That’s useful to know for businesses with only a small number of reviews.
4. Could there be an option to disable photos?It would be very interesting if there were a feature to turn photos off.

That could make blog production free or at least much easier to scale weekly.Then it might also become possible to create blogs based more on website content and public internet information about the business and its sector, instead of relying mainly on reviews and the Google Business Profile.

I think that would make the feature a lot more flexible.

title: 🚧 This Week’s Development Plan
content:
Hi Climbers,

here’s what we’re focusing on this week.

We’re starting to work on the employee tracking feature, based on all the feedback you shared in the community over the past days. The goal is to build a system that allows businesses to involve their team in the review collection process, track performance, and create a healthy, gamified competition between employees. Thanks again for all the insights — they’ve been very helpful in shaping how we approach this feature.

At the same time, we’ll also work on a reorganization of the Client Mode menu UI.

The idea is to make the platform even simpler and more intuitive for local business owners by grouping all features into 3 main areas: Reputation, Visibility, and Engagement.

This is purely a UI improvement, so no changes in functionality — just a cleaner structure to help clients better understand where everything is and how to use it.

The goal, as always, is to make the platform as easy as possible to use for non-tech users, while keeping all the power under the hood.

The Climbo Team 🚀

title: It took some time but then 💥BOOOM💥
content:
Cheerio Climbers,
I started dealing with Climbo 2,5 months ago. I set up my website and watched most of the tutorials. Super hyped I contacted businesses in my surroundings \(I focus on restaurants and cafés\). To be honest: It was draining. Most restaurant owner were not very pleased to listen to my offer - after they've been contacted by Google and other local business directories before. Good luck to all of you who push through initial challenges as well. Keep going 🙌

title: 🧗 Welcome To The Climbo Accelerator — Start Here
content:
Welcome to the Climbo Accelerator!

This space is built for marketing agency owners who want to add a real, long-term SaaS business line to their agency using Climbo. The goal is simple: help you generate predictable monthly recurring revenue while delivering concrete, measurable results to your local business clients.

The Classroom is your structured learning hub. To get started, head to 👉 [https://www.skool.com/climbo/classroom](https://www.skool.com/climbo/classroom) and begin with the “Start Here” course. This course explains what Climbo is, how the platform works, and—most importantly—how your agency can use it to create new MRR by upselling it to your existing local business clients. It will give you the strategic foundation you need before touching the product or speaking to clients.

Once you’ve completed the Start Here course, activate your Climbo account and move on to the “5-Day Implementation Plan” course. This course is entirely practical. It walks you step by step through setting up your Climbo account correctly during the first 5 days—white-labeling the platform, connecting Stripe, creating your pricing plans, and testing the full client onboarding flow—so you can start selling immediately and see real results from the platform as quickly as possible.

Inside the Community, everything is organized into a few clear areas. Updates is where we share product announcements, feature releases, new resources, and important news about Climbo. Wins is the space where members share progress, milestones, and results—whether it’s onboarding a first client or hitting a new MRR goal. Gems is dedicated to high-value insights, strategies, tools, and lessons learned that can help others move faster. Support is for questions that may be useful to the community as a whole, while account-specific issues should always be handled through the support ticket form inside the Climbo platform. Intros is where you introduce yourself, share your background, and explain your goals, helping create real connections and collaboration. Feel free to introduce yourself in the Intros category — tell us who you are, what kind of agency you run, and what you’re aiming to build.

title: GEO Agent as a newsletter engine
content:
We should expand the GEO Agent beyond blog content and position it as a newsletter generation engine.

Instead of focusing only on SEO articles, the GEO Agent could transform business data — especially customer reviews — into ready-to-send newsletters that businesses can use to stay top of mind with their audience.

Most local businesses don’t have the time or resources to consistently communicate with their customers. At the same time, they are already sitting on valuable content: real customer feedback.

By turning reviews into newsletters, we unlock a new distribution channel that is:

- Easier to consume than blog content
- More direct \(reaches customers via email or WhatsApp\)
- Consistent and automated
- Aligned with real customer experiences

Instead of just helping businesses “be found,” we help them “stay present.”

title: Review Links Ignoring Device/Account Language Settings
content:
We’ve identified a critical issue with the review link behavior in Kiosk Mode that we’d like to bring to everyone’s attention.
In our case, users are scanning the QR code using Safari with their device language set to Spanish. However, the review page consistently opens in English instead of respecting the user’s language or the account settings.
For markets like ours \(or others besides english\), this is not a minor detail, it’s a key part of the user experience. In fact, one of our clients has already asked us to pause the rollout entirely until this is resolved.
Has anyone found a workaround or a way to force the review link / QR code to respect the selected language?
This is extremely important for international adoption, and we’d really appreciate any insights or updates from the team 🙏

title: Some Information about AEO
content:
This a file I'm sharing from another training program I'm a member of.
Answer Engine Optimization \(AEO\) is the process of structuring content to be easily interpreted, cited, and delivered by AI-powered tools like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Perplexity. Unlike SEO, which aims for top keyword rankings, AEO focuses on providing direct, conversational, and authoritative answers to user queries, aiming for citations, mentions, and increased visibility in AI-generated, zero-click search results.

As someone whose business relies heavily on WordPress sites and plugins, I made the switch from Zapier to Ottokit a while back, and it's been a game-changer. The AI Agents have unlocked incredible flexibility for handling customer interactions—think automated responses, smart content generation, and adaptive flows that just make life easier. On top of that, Ottokit is more budget-friendly. Their FREE plan alone gives you 20 full workflows \(that's like supporting 20 customers with unlimited "zaps" running in the background\)—no hidden fees or task limits holding you back.

If you're familiar with Zapier, you'll feel right at home: both are no-code platforms with super similar interfaces and setups. The learning curve is basically nonexistent, so you can dive in and start automating without the hassle.

title: Apple is building a “local business platform”. What does that mean for us?
content:
Apple just launched Apple Business \(April 14\) and I think it’s something we should keep on our radar for Climbo [@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf).

This is not Apple becoming a full reputation management platform \(yet\). It’s mainly Apple Business Connect + Apple Business Manager + business email/calendar/directory + device management, plus a big focus on local discoverability across Apple Maps, Siri, Spotlight, Wallet, Mail, etc.

The part that matters for usis that Apple is making it easier for businesses to manage their Apple Maps presence \(place cards, photos, hours, categories, showcases/offers, actions like order/reserve\) and they’re introducing Apple Maps ads \(starting this summer, US/Canada only\).

Yes, just like the Google Business Profile we all know.

So thinking ahead, knowing how big Apple is worldwide, I see this as an opportunity for Climbo to stand out by supporting Apple \(from the beginning\) as a channel. Similar to GBP being the #1 channel today.

The roadmap ideas I think could be usefull are:
[ol:1][li]Add an “Apple Maps / Apple Business Profile” per location \(like the Google Profile feature\). Show if the Apple listing is claimed, and manage/track the key place card info \(name, address, hours, photos, category, website link, etc.\).[li]Add Apple Maps insights to the dashboard \(like visibility stats, not just reviews\). Show basic Apple Maps performance like searches/views and taps on call/website/directions \(even if it starts with manual input/import\).[li]Add Apple as a review channel inside Climbo \(monitoring + replying workflow\). If Apple reviews are available and integration is possible: monitor new Apple reviews and help reply. If not possible yet: at least a link-out button + task/reminder workflow \(“new Apple review → go reply”\).

Even though it’s US/Canada first, I think we should watch far ahead. If Apple pushes this globally, the platforms that move early will stay ahead of the curve, and if we decide it’s needed, we can likely add Apple Maps support faster than most reputation management platforms out there.

title: How to Actually Start – $5K MRR Climber
content:
Hi Climbers,

I want to highlight this approach shared in the community by [@Gary Hickman](obj://user/11edae37f7014bb693caa6389a3589f9) because it perfectly shows how this business really works in the real world.

The idea is simple, but very powerful.

Instead of overthinking pricing, branding, funnels, or trying to make everything perfect from day one… just start by getting results for a few businesses.

Go after businesses that can clearly afford your service \(for example $400–$600/month clients\). Then offer them a free trial for a few months and go all-in.

Set everything up for them. Help them collect reviews. Even invest a bit upfront if needed \(flyers, NFC cards, QR codes\). The goal is not to make money immediately, but to create real case studies and real impact.

At that point, you’re no longer an “external tool”.
You become part of their team.

You manage their Google and Facebook profiles, you monitor their reviews, and most importantly… you start seeing problems:

• slow responses to reviews
• missed opportunities
• bad ratings not handled properly
• no consistent review flow

And that’s where the real opportunity is.

When you go back to them \(even just for a coffee\), you’re not pitching anymore. You’re showing them what’s happening and where they’re losing money.

Then you offer the solution.

This is the key shift:
from selling software → to solving real problems.

And you don’t need many clients to make this work.

Even just 2–3 clients at $400–$600/month already makes the whole thing worthwhile and validates your business.

The takeaway is this:

Start small.
Get hands-on.
Focus on results.
Use those results to sell.

This is how you build a real reputation management business.

title: Enhancement Request: Interactive Job Mapping Layer
content:
Climbo already does something powerful—you capture customer feedback and build trust through reviews.
But right now, that trust lives mostly in words, sometimes supported by video.
What’s missing is a visual and geographic proof layer that shows where and how that service actually happens.

At the moment, Climbo represents one side of the story—the customer’s voice.

By introducing this additional layer, you give service providers the ability to actively contribute to their credibility, enriching their online footprint with real, location-based proof of work.

Reviews answer:
“Was the service good?”

Interactive Job Mapping Layer answers:
“Where and what was actually done?”
Together, they complete the story.

Visual Proof of Service Delivery - a map-based feature that allows businesses to pin completed jobs, showing exactly where they’ve worked, what they did, and proof of it.

Example: A plumber completes a job in blah blah.
They drop a pin: “Blocked Drain – Rhode Island”
Add a photo + short note.
Now anyone can SEE real work in that exact area.

This gives Climbo:
✔ Higher user engagement \(maps are interactive\)
✔ More content creation \(every job becomes data\)
✔ Stronger SEO value \(location + service signals\)
✔ Clear product differentiation
✔ A premium feature opportunity

Climbo already owns the voice of the customer. This allows you to own the proof of the work—on the same platform.

IN ADDITION: How this aligns with Google \(Relevance, Distance, Prominence\)
Google ranks local businesses based on three core factors:

Relevance
How well a business matches what someone is searching for.
Interactive Job Mapping Layer strengthens this by linking:
[ul][li]Service + Specific location\(e.g., “Drain cleaning – Thesen Islands”\)

Distance
How close the business is to the searcher.
Interactive Job Mapping Layer supports this by:
[ul][li]Showing activity across multiple areas[li]Expanding perceived service coverage

Prominence
How trusted and established a business is.

title: Rating average calculation
content:
Some rating average calculations are confusing on the statistics dashboard.
I've opened a discussion with the support about it but thought it'd be interesting to bring it up here.

For Trustpilot, they use what they call a TrustScore which is not a plain average but has all sorts of other criterias: [https://help.trustpilot.com/s/article/TrustScore-and-star-rating-explained?language=en_US](https://help.trustpilot.com/s/article/TrustScore-and-star-rating-explained?language=en_US)
If this TrustScore is used in Climbo, we end up with statistics like "1 five star review = 3.7 average rating" which is very confusing.
I would suggest either to rename it in "TrustPilot TrustScore" if we use their number \(and maybe with a link to their documentation\) or use a real average calculation.

For Facebook, the ratings are only in "thumbs up"/"thumbs down" so we don't have a rating based on five stars but a percentage of positive reviews. When I read the numbers of the demo account, it doesn't make any sense \(don't know if it's because it's the demo account and the numbers are not really calculated\):
35 reviews - 5% positives \(only 2 over 35?\)
you need 25 positive reviews to reach 6%...
Facebook displays a five star based rating in Google Search results, I think it would be easier to understand and more uniform with other systems to figure out and use their method of calculation to display this five star rating.

Maybe you have other examples from other platforms.

Currently, all of this makes the statistics dashboard difficult to understand. What are your thoughts on it?

title: Development Request: Review → Reward Flow
content:
We require the implementation of a post-review gamification trigger within the review journey to encourage engagement. Incentivize review acquisition!
What is required:
Enable a flow where, immediately after a user submits a review \(via link or QR entry\), a reward interaction is automatically triggered \(e.g. spin wheel / coupon / loyalty activation\).

Why this is critical:
[ul][li]Reviews are a primary ranking signal for Google Business Profiles — volume and frequency directly impact visibility.[li]Current behaviour shows users need prompting + incentive to complete reviews.[li]Introducing a post-action reward significantly increases completion rates without influencing review sentiment.[li]The trigger must occur after submission and not be tied to rating, ensuring platform compliance and unbiased feedback.

Outcome:
This creates a high-conversion loop:
Access → Review → Immediate Reward → Increased participation
Driving:
[ul][li]More consistent review generation[li]Higher engagement rates[li]Improved local search performance

title: Feature Request: SEO agent smart Negative Review Handling for High-Volume Businesses🚀
content:
Hi team,

This is a feature request coming from multiple clients.

Right now, auto-replies work well for general reviews.
But for businesses receiving hundreds of reviews per month or quarter, negative reviews are a different case.

Those cannot always be handled with a normal public reply.
In many cases, complaints need to be moved to email so the issue can be solved properly by the support team.

The problem is:
right now, clients still have to handle this manually, which is not scalable.

A very valuable solution would be one of these:

[ol:1][li]The AI detects when a review should be handled as a complaint and automatically replies with a support-style response, asking the customer to continue via a specific email address.[li]A separate prompt can be created for low-rated reviews, for example 1 to 3 stars, with different instructions and a support email included.

Personally, option 1 would be the strongest, because it gives the AI more flexibility and makes the workflow smarter.

Would something like this be possible to build?Het

title: Development Request: Enable Gamification & Custom HTML Integration
content:
In yesterday's AMA I asked for HTML inclusion in TEXTS, and was asked to table my request and reasoning here....This enables us to incorporate gamification on our own

Objective
Requesting the ability to implement a post-review engagement mechanism that introduces gamification into the user journey.

Preferred Approach \(Primary Request\)
Ideally, we would like to see native gamification functionality built directly into Climbo, such as:
[ul][li]Spin-to-win mechanics[li]Coupon or reward unlocking[li]Loyalty-based incentives

This would allow for a fully integrated, seamless user experience within the platform.

Alternative Approach \(Critical Fallback\)
If native gamification is not currently feasible, we request the ability to:
✔ Embed custom HTML / JavaScript within the post-review \(thank-you\) page
✔ Trigger delayed interactions \(e.g. gamification after X seconds\)
✔ Control post-click flow \(redirects, overlays, modals\)
✔ Integrate with external systems \(CouponTools, loyalty platforms, reward engines\)

This ensures we can still deliver the required functionality without being constrained by platform limitations.

Current Limitation
At present, the system:
[ul][li]Does not allow HTML or script injection[li]Does not support post-action event triggers[li]Does not allow control over timing or sequencing after review interaction

This prevents the creation of a review → reward behavioural loop.

Why This Matters
This request is rooted in performance and behavioural design:
[ul][li]Reviews are a primary ranking factor for Google Business Profiles[li]Users require prompting and motivation to complete reviews[li]Gamification significantly increases engagement and completion rates[li]A structured post-action flow creates a repeatable and scalable system

Strategic Outcome
Enabling either native gamification or HTML flexibility allows us to build:
Access → Review → Timed Reward → Ongoing Engagement

Driving:
[ul][li]Increased review volume and consistency[li]Higher engagement rates[li]Improved local search visibility

title: 🚧 This Week’s Focus: GEO Agent + Mobile App A.M.A.
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

This week, we’re starting work on one of the most exciting features we’ve been planning: the GEO Agent.

The goal of the GEO Agent is simple:

👉 automatically generate SEO-optimized, localized content for your clients based on their real business data.

Here’s how it will work:

We start from the input layer, where the system gathers all the key business information — business name, category, services, description, sentiment analysis, and especially real customer reviews.

Then the agent analyzes all this data to understand how the business is perceived, what makes it unique, and which topics matter most in its local market.

From there, it generates relevant keywords, followed by tailored topics and content ideas specific to that business and its location.

Once everything is ready, the agent creates content on a schedule — weekly, biweekly, or monthly — selecting the most relevant topics and generating human-like articles designed to support local SEO. Reviews play a key role in this process, since the agent can also quote real customer feedback inside the articles to make the content more authentic, relevant, and aligned with how the business is actually experienced by its customers.

👉 The final output:

The main content is published as a blog article on the client’s website \(CNAME required\).

Optionally, the same content can also be adapted into social media posts for Facebook, Instagram, and Google, with the goal of driving traffic back to the blog article and amplifying its reach across multiple channels.

Every few months, the system refreshes the input data to keep everything aligned with the latest reviews and business updates.

This is a big step toward helping you offer not just reputation management, but also automated local SEO content powered by real customer data.

📲 Reminder — Mobile App AMA \(Tomorrow\)

Don’t forget: tomorrow we have a special AMA dedicated to the mobile app service.

We’ll show how you can take your SaaS to the next level with your own branded iOS & Android app.

title: 🚀 New Features Released This Week – A.M.A. Recording (26/03/2026)
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

Here’s what we’ve just shipped this week: a mix of new features, improvements, and important fixes.

We’ve introduced Kiosk Mode Download. You can now easily print and use it in physical locations like counters, desks, or waiting areas to collect more reviews offline.

The review experience is now even more global with automatic translations on the review link, adapting to the customer’s browser language to increase conversion across different countries.

We’ve also added email reminders, so if a customer doesn’t act after the first request, they’ll automatically receive a follow-up after a chosen delay — helping you recover missed opportunities.

A small but useful improvement: SMS character counter, so you can better control message length and avoid unexpected charges.

On the prospecting side, you can now generate printable reports, perfect for in-person meetings or door-to-door outreach.

We’ve also introduced next week's plan for the GEO Agent; in the comments, the displayed workflow.

🔧 Fixes

• Users can no longer create multiple accounts during the free trial
• Widgets will now automatically turn off if the subscription ends

📲 Reminder

Don’t forget the Mobile App A.M.A. \(31st March, 4 pm Italian time\) — where we’ll show how to take your SaaS to the next level with your own iOS & Android app: [https://www.skool.com/climbo/calendar?eid=85ec016676ab43a38f2f90ee989223c5](https://www.skool.com/climbo/calendar?eid=85ec016676ab43a38f2f90ee989223c5)

As always, thanks for all your feedback — keep it coming 🙌

title: 🚀 New Features Released — AMA Recording (19/02/2025)
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

Today we released a big batch of improvements across Client Mode, Agency Mode and API, all shown during the AMA.

A lot of these come directly from your feedback to make the platform easier to use, more flexible for your offers, and better connected with your workflows.

Client Mode improvements

You can now reorder the review platforms on the review link so you can push the platform that matters most for your client first.

Video testimonials can be displayed directly inside widgets and we also added the Leave a Review button inside widgets to increase conversions from websites.

We added the ability to change a client login email without recreating the account, safely disconnect a website domain, and delete scheduled Google posts.

Google Sheets integration is in progress and will allow automatic contact syncing without external tools.

Agency Mode improvements

You can now customize platform colors, enable the AI chatbot for clients, and allows coupons on specific plans and also apply them to existing customers.

The new webhook builder lets you connect Climbo to any external system or automation flow directly from the platform.

API & integrations

We added new webhooks, new upcoming Zapier events, invite links returned in the “Get Client” response, and the ability to set a password when adding a client \(skipping the onboarding password step\).

Thanks again for continuously sharing feedback — it’s literally shaping the platform every week 🙌

See you in the next AMA!

title: 🙌 New Features Released – AMA Recording (19/03/2026)
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

We’ve just rolled out a new set of features focused on improving usability, flexibility, and giving you more control over both your clients and your sales process.

You can now use the new clients filter in the clients section to quickly find accounts by name, email, status, or plan. This makes managing a growing number of clients much faster and more organized.

We’ve also introduced demo by category, allowing you to launch different demo accounts based on specific industries. This is a big upgrade for your sales process, as you can now show prospects a demo that feels tailored to their business \(restaurant, dentist, salon, etc.\), making your pitch much more relevant and impactful.

Another important update is related to messaging costs. With SMS and WhatsApp pricing per country, you now pay exactly what we are charged by Bird and Meta, with full transparency depending on the destination country.

We’ve also improved the AI experience. You can now use your SEO Agent and Social Agent settings for manual AI actions, meaning that even when generating replies or captions manually, the AI will follow the tone, style, and instructions you’ve already configured.

Finally, we’ve added Google booking links integration. You can now connect a booking link directly to your client’s Google Business Profile as the main CTA \(visible in search results\), and the same CTA will automatically appear on the pre-built website. This helps drive more conversions by turning visibility into actual bookings.

As always, these updates come directly from your feedback — keep it coming 🙌

title: 🚧 This Week’s Plan + Mobile App A.M.A.
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

This week, we’re focusing on a few important features that were already partially developed so we can fully complete them and avoid leaving anything half done. The goal is to wrap everything up now so that, starting next week, we can focus entirely on the GEO agent without distractions.

Here’s what we’re working on this week:

We’re adding email reminders to increase review conversion. SMS and WhatsApp reminders are already live, and now email will be added as well. If a customer doesn’t click on the review link after the first request, they’ll automatically receive a follow-up after a selected number of days. It’s a simple feature, but one that can make a big difference in results.

We’re also introducing downloadable kiosk mode. You’ll be able to customize and print your review link kiosk, then use it physically in-store — perfect for desks, counters, and other high-traffic areas.

Another important improvement is automatic translations on the review link, even after the text has been edited. The experience will automatically adapt to the user’s browser language, making it much easier to collect reviews from clients who speak different languages and helping improve conversion globally.

We’re making the prospecting reports printable, so it becomes easier to use during in-person meetings or whenever you want to show a prospect a clear physical version of the report.

We’re also working on the final pieces needed to complete our new mobile app service for those who want a branded iOS and Android app published in the stores. This will be a great option for anyone who wants to offer an even stronger software experience under their own brand.

On top of that, we’re making some UI improvements for the mobile version of client mode, so clients can enjoy using the platform from their phones more and more. The goal is to make the mobile experience smoother, clearer, and more enjoyable overall.

🤳 Special A.M.A. — Mobile App

We’ve planned a special live A.M.A. on March 31 at 4:00 PM \(Italian time\), focused entirely on the mobile app.

title: 🔧 This Week’s Focus: Platform Review & Improvements
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

This week we’re taking a short pause from releasing new features to focus on something equally important: reviewing and refining the entire platform.

Over the last 6 months, we’ve developed and released a huge number of features, shipping updates every single week. Because of this fast pace, we want to take a few days to step back and go through the platform carefully, reviewing the code, fixing minor bugs, and making small UI improvements where needed.

The goal is simple: make sure the platform is as solid, stable, and smooth as possible for you and your clients.

Sometimes the most important work is not adding more features, but making sure everything already built works perfectly and feels polished.

From next week we’ll go back to our usual development rhythm and continue releasing new features.

Thanks again for all the feedback you keep sharing — it’s helping us improve the platform every single week 🙌

title: How to Enable AI-Assisted Review Suggestions to Improve Google Search Visibility
content:
A frequently overlooked feature can significantly help your clients improve their visibility in Google search results. Google’s algorithm often uses keywords found in customer reviews to understand and rank businesses. By guiding customers to include relevant service and location keywords in their reviews, you can strengthen a business’s local SEO performance.

Follow the steps below to configure this feature.

Enable AI Review Suggestions:
[ol:1][li]Log in to your client’s dashboard.[li]Navigate to Settings.[li]Select Link Review.[li]In the Link Review section, locate the dropdown menu labeled AI Suggestion.[li]Turn AI Suggestion ON.

Add Evaluation Criteria: To guide customers in writing meaningful reviews:
[ol:1][li]Add 3–5 Evaluation Criteria.[li]Each criterion should include a short description of the service being rated.[li]Customers will rate each criterion using a 5-star satisfaction scale.
You may generate these criteria using AI tools such as ChatGPT. Example prompt:
“I have a landing page to rate \[Type in business name\] services before customers leave a review, and an AI tool that helps them write better reviews. I need to create evaluation criteria and brief descriptions for each service we provide. Customers will rate their satisfaction using a 5-star rating.”

Add Target Keywords
[ol:1][li]Insert relevant keywords related to the business services.[li]Include location-based keywords such as the city, town, or service area.[li]You may create these manually or generate them using AI tools. \(use your own keywords or use ChatGPT to give you keyword related to the business services and LOCATION town or area, you have to provide to your AI prompt\). Each keyword you have to add separately.[li]Click Save once finished.

Configure the Review Suggestion Text:
[ol:1][li]From the top dropdown menu, select Text.[li]Go to Review Suggestion.[li]In the field "Label for Additional Notes", enter: Additional comments[li]In the field "Placeholder for Additional Notes", enter:

title: 🚀 Feature request: test the SEO Agent directly inside the reviews screen
content:
I want to share this feature request again because I think it would be really valuable for a lot of users. 👇

Right now, inside the reviews screen, you can test replies with the normal AI using options like friendly, empathetic, short, medium, or long.

But what is still missing is the ability to test the SEO Agent there as well.

And I think that is important.

A lot of clients simply want to know how the SEO Agent will respond before they turn it on.
They want to see the style, the wording, and whether it uses the setup correctly.

At the moment, that is not possible.

You can test the normal AI, but you cannot test the SEO Agent itself with the selected source, keywords, and prompt.
So clients cannot clearly see what they are enabling before switching it on.

I think there should be an option directly inside the reviews screen, where you normally see all reviews, to click on the SEO Agent and generate a test reply there as well.

That test reply should use:

[ul][li]the selected source[li]the selected keywords[li]the configured prompt

Without posting the reply live.

This would make it much easier to:

✅ show clients exactly how the SEO Agent responds
✅ test whether the setup is correct before activation
✅ adjust the source, keywords, or prompt if needed
✅ build more trust during onboarding and demos

I also think it would be very valuable if, in the future, there could be a separate prompt setup for negative reviews.

Because a 1 to 3 star review usually needs a very different response than a positive one.

So ideally:

⭐ positive reviews → their own prompt/setup
⚠️ negative reviews → separate prompt/setup

I think this would be a very logical and useful improvement.
Curious how others here think about this. 👀

title: 🚀 New Features Released – A.M.A. Recording (12/03/2026)
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

Here are some of the updates we’ve worked on and released this week.

We’ve introduced review request reminders. If a contact receives a review request but does not click the review link, the system can now automatically send a follow-up reminder after a selected number of days. The reminder message is fully editable, giving you complete control while helping increase the chances of turning more contacts into reviews. At the moment, review request reminders are available only for text messaging channels \(SMS and WhatsApp\), while Email reminders will be released soon. Reminders can be enabled at the plan level.

Another new feature is custom sidebar links in Client Mode, which can be assigned to specific plans. This means you can display certain links only to clients on selected plans, such as a Gold plan. These links can point to any URL you choose, including premium services, extra resources, booking pages, upsells, or support portals.

We’re also continuing to expand the unbranded knowledge base by adding more tutorial videos that you can safely share with your clients. The goal is to build a growing library of clean, unbranded resources that help clients understand how to use the platform and its features, while keeping the software completely white-label from their perspective.

As always, these updates are driven by your feedback, so keep sharing ideas in the community 🙌

title: I already have a QR code" – the objection that kills?
content:
Hey everyone!

I bet this has happened to you: you pitch your solution to boost Google reviews and they hit you with:

"Oh but I already have a QR code, I don't need another one!"

Frustrating, right? Out of my last 10 prospects, they all said it.

But I figured something out: the problem isn't the QR code, it's what's behind it.

Here's what I tell them now to make them think:

The QR code is just a door: A regular QR code sends people to a link. Mine is connected to software that automates everything, filters reviews, and personalizes the experience.

What do you say to this objection?

Thanks for your help
Claude

title: 🚧 This Week’s Development Focus – Reminders Are Coming!
content:
This week we’re continuing to push forward on several improvements and work-in-progress features based on your feedback.

We’re still actively developing some of the features currently in progress, especially the demo account by categories and the Google Sheets integration. Both are moving forward and we’ll keep refining them until they’re ready for release.

We’re also expanding the unbranded knowledge base, adding more tutorial videos that you can safely share with your clients. The goal is to give you a growing library of clean, unbranded resources that help your clients understand the platform and its features without exposing the underlying software.

Another important feature we’re working on is review request reminders. If a contact receives a review request via email, SMS, or WhatsApp but does not click the review link, the platform will be able to automatically send a follow-up reminder after a chosen number of days. The reminder text will be editable, giving you more control while increasing the chances of getting that review.

We’re also building custom sidebar links in Client Mode tied to specific plans. This will allow you to show certain links only to clients on specific plans \(for example Gold\). Those links can redirect to any URL you want, such as premium services, extra resources, booking pages, or upsells.

And as always, we’re continuing to improve the prospecting tool based on your constant feedback. Many of you are actively using it in real sales situations, so we want to keep making it more practical, clearer, and more powerful for generating conversations with prospects.

Thanks again for all the feedback you keep sharing in the community — it’s literally shaping the product every week 🙌

title: Networking Events - GDPR (🇬🇧)
content:
Hi Climbers,
I was at a local networking meeting this Friday just gone, the first one for a very long time, but also the first since GDPR came into effect.
I got some great contacts on paper, and I asked Gemini how and what I should do to stay compliant.

Here is the advice:
Managing a physical list of contacts after a networking event is a classic "analog meets digital" privacy challenge. Since you’re handling Personal Identifiable Information \(PII\), GDPR definitely applies.
The good news? GDPR isn't meant to stop you from networking; it’s meant to ensure you do it transparently. Here is a step-by-step guide to staying compliant while turning those scribbles into professional relationships.
1. Establish Your "Lawful Basis"
Under GDPR, you need a legal reason to process data. For networking, you generally rely on two:
* Consent: They explicitly wrote their name down for a specific purpose \(e.g., "Sign up for my newsletter"\).
* Legitimate Interest: You met at a professional event, exchanged info, and there is a reasonable expectation that you will follow up regarding business.
2. Secure the Physical Data
That notepad is now a "filing system."
* Keep it safe: Don't leave the pad in your car or at a coffee shop.
* Digitize and Destroy: Once you transfer the names into your CRM or email contacts, shred the paper or store it in a locked drawer. Do not just toss it in the recycling bin.
3. The "First Touch" Protocol
When you send that first email, you must provide specific information to remain compliant:
* Identify yourself: State clearly who you are and where you met.
* State the purpose: Tell them why you are emailing \(e.g., "Following up on our chat about AI consulting"\).
* Provide an Out: Always include a way for them to say "no thanks."
4. Avoid the "Auto-Enroll" Trap
This is the most common GDPR slip-up. Meeting someone at a networking event is not a blanket permission to add them to a marketing drip campaign or a mass newsletter.
* Personal Follow-up: Totally fine.

title: Trying something different instead of cold calling local businesses
content:
Cold calling never felt like a good fit for me, so I started experimenting with a different approach.

Instead of calling random businesses, I started looking for companies with unusually strong review patterns. Not just star ratings, but the language customers use.

When I see something interesting, I write a short feature about them on a local discovery site I'm building. I’ve written a few so far and have a growing list of candidates.

The idea is simple: start the relationship with recognition instead of a pitch.

Still early, but I’m curious whether highlighting businesses first might lead to better conversations later about reputation tools.

I’ll report back once I have tangible results.

When you read reviews, what makes you think “this business is doing something right”?

And when you see a business already doing well, do you see that as a better prospect… or one that’s less likely to need help?

title: 🚀 New Features Released + AMA Recording (05/03/2026)
content:
Hi Climbers,

We’ve just released a new batch of updates, and the recording of yesterday’s AMA is now available below this post.

This week’s release is focused on making the platform easier to customize, more compliant, and more professional in the eyes of your clients.

We added the ability to edit request templates more cleanly, both for email and SMS, so you have more control over the content you send. We also introduced buttons inside emails, making review requests look much more polished and improving click-through compared to raw links.

A big part of this release is the new fully compliant review link. The flow has been redesigned so every customer can always choose between leaving a public review or private feedback, while still keeping the “smart” ordering of options when the review filter is enabled. This makes the link safer, clearer, and aligned with current expectations. On top of that, you can now decide from your custom plans whether specific review link features are enabled or not.

We also refreshed the design of the sentiment analysis section and the competitor chart, making them easier to read and more impactful during demos. Another nice improvement: your demos now open directly in the browser language, which makes the experience much smoother for international clients.

title: The Secret to Near-Perfect Reviews: The WhatsApp Intercept 🛡️
content:
Public reviews are high stakes. Once a 1-star review is live, it’s permanent. The smartest way to manage your reputation isn't just asking for reviews it's controlling the environment where those reviews happen.
How This Workflow Protects Your Business
By using a Scan-to-WhatsApp flow, you stop "raw" feedback from hitting your public profiles immediately.
* The Private Filter: Instead of a direct link to Google, the customer lands in a private WhatsApp chat.
* Conflict Resolution: If a customer had a bad experience, they vent to you directly. You can fix the issue in real-time before they ever think about hitting "Post" on a public platform.
* The "Happy Path" Only: Once a customer confirms they are satisfied in the chat, then you automate the link to your public review page.
Why It’s a Game Changer for Physical Locations:
* Intercept Discontent: Turn a potential 1-star public review into a private customer service win.
* Stay Compliant: You aren't "review gating" in a way that breaks terms of service; you are simply providing a superior, direct communication channel.
* High Conversion: People are much more likely to click a link in a familiar WhatsApp thread than an automated email that lands in their "Promotions" tab.
> The Strategy: Don't leave your reputation to chance. Use WhatsApp as a "Front Desk" for feedback. Fix the problems privately, and broadcast the wins publicly.

title: Feature Request: AI Test Mode + Automatic Language Detection
content:
Hi,

I’ve noticed that clients hesitate to activate the AI agent for automatic review replies, especially on existing reviews.

The reason is simple:
they can’t see in advance how the AI will actually respond to their specific reviews.

Would it be possible to add a test feature?

For example:
– A “Test AI response” button
– Where you can select an existing review
– And let the AI generate a response in a test environment \(without posting it live\)

That way, I can show clients a demo:
“This is how the AI would respond to your reviews.”

This would significantly lower the barrier.
Right now, some clients are afraid that once the AI is activated, it will automatically start replying to 5 old reviews per day without them knowing what the tone will look like beforehand.

With a test mode, they can:
– Evaluate the quality
– Adjust prompts if needed
– Build trust
– Go live with more confidence

I believe this would also increase conversion rates for agencies.

Additionally, one more question:
When is the automatic language detection and translation feature coming?
Meaning that the AI automatically detects whether a review is in Dutch, English, German, etc., and replies in the same language.

That would make it even stronger for international use.

title: Request🚨‼️: Detailed review statistics dashboard (like Climbo 1.0)
content:
Hi,

I would like to request a feature that many agencies and clients find extremely important: a clear statistics dashboard showing the distribution of review ratings and growth over time.

Right now we can see how many reviews came in per month, but this does not give enough insight into the actual performance of the review system.

What would be very valuable is a dashboard that shows:

• Star distribution \(how many 1★, 2★, 3★, 4★, 5★ reviews\)
• Average rating growth over time
• Number of reviews gained over time
• Platform distribution \(Google, Trustpilot, etc.\)
• Negative feedback collected internally

And most importantly:

Clients should be able to filter by time period such as:

• Last 30 days
• Last 3 months
• Last 12 months
• All time

This allows agencies and clients to clearly see:

• how their rating improved
• how many new reviews were generated
• how the star distribution changes over time

This was something that worked very well in Climbo 1.0, and I think especially now that review rules are becoming stricter \(Google review gating policies\), the focus should shift even more toward high-quality review analytics and transparency.

Many clients care a lot about these insights because it shows the real impact of the system, not just the number of invitations sent.

I have also seen multiple users request this type of dashboard before, so I think it would add a lot of value to the platform.

Thanks!

title: 🚨 I genuinely think this could be a big opportunity for Climbo.
content:
I was talking to an entrepreneur recently who wanted the official Trustpilot widget on his website. The real one. With the recognizable green Trustpilot branding, the original star style, the clear average score, and the visible total review count.

But to use that, he needs a paid Trustpilot Business subscription. And that’s expensive.

So we looked at our widget instead.

His feedback was honest:
“It works… but it doesn’t look premium.”

And that’s where I see the opportunity.

Right now, our popup widget appears every few seconds in the corner with a review, which is great. But what we don’t have yet is a strong, premium embedded widget that really feels high-end on modern websites.

Here’s the idea:

First, a real auto-scrolling review widget for websites.
Not just a static block. Not just something you click through. But a smooth, automatically moving review section. Reviews sliding or fading in one after another. Adjustable speed. Clean animation. Something that feels like those professional logo or partnership sections that move automatically on high-end sites.

That alone would massively upgrade the perceived value.

Second, platform-specific widgets in the exact recognizable style.

For example, a true Trustpilot-style widget:

[ul][li]The official green Trustpilot look[li]The recognizable Trustpilot star design[li]The logo clearly visible[li]The average rating displayed prominently[li]The total number of reviews clearly shown

Not just “green stars”. But the real visual experience people associate with Trustpilot.

Same for TripAdvisor:

[ul][li]Their recognizable layout[li]Their score presentation style[li]Their visual identity

Why is this important?

Because recognition builds trust instantly. When people see that familiar Trustpilot or TripAdvisor style, it feels credible immediately.

From a sales perspective, this becomes a very strong angle. You can tell a client:

“You don’t need to pay for an expensive Trustpilot Business subscription just to have a premium-looking review widget. You can get a high-end version through us.”

title: 🚧 This Week’s Focus: Review Link, Plan Controls & Ongoing Improvements
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

This week we’re working on two important areas: giving you more control from custom plans, and making the review link fully aligned with Google’s expectations.

First, you’ll be able to enable or disable specific review link features directly from the custom plan — for example AI Review Suggestions and the initial review filter — so you can decide exactly what experience each client receives depending on your positioning.

Now the key topic: the review filter and the current review link experience.

We’ve read all your comments and we want to clarify what actually matters for Google. The rule is simple — if you ask a customer for a review, every customer must always have a real possibility to leave a public review, positive or negative. The issue is not collecting private feedback: the issue is blocking or discouraging public reviews.

Right now, in the negative experience page the “leave a public review” option exists, but it’s much smaller than the private feedback button. In practice this creates an imbalance: customers are naturally pushed toward leaving private feedback instead of having an equal choice. Even if the public option is technically present, it does not have the same visibility.

We are fixing this.

In the new version, both options will have equal importance. Customers will clearly see they can either send private feedback or leave a public review on Google \(or other platforms\), with properly visible buttons for each platform. The goal is not to direct the decision, but to let the customer genuinely choose.

In the positive experience page, they will also have both options: public review or private feedback.
If the filter is enabled, only the order changes:

low rating → private first, public second
high rating → public first, private second

If the filter is disabled, the experience becomes a single neutral page where customers freely choose between public review and private feedback.

We also discussed this structure with operators in the space who have directly interacted with Google, and this aligns with the guidelines.

title: 🚀 New Features - AMA Recording (26/02/2026)
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

This week we focused on one thing as always: make the platform easier for your clients to understand, easier for them to use, and therefore easier for you to sell and keep them long term.

We introduced AI Review Suggestions. Customers will answer simple evaluation criteria you define \(rated 1-5 plus optional note\) and the AI generates a ready-to-post review including selected keywords. They just copy it and choose where to publish it \(Google, Facebook, etc.\). The goal is removing friction — many happy customers simply don’t know what to write.

We also redesigned the Reviews vs Requests UI. Reviews is now only for reading and replying. Requests is the single place where reviews are asked. No more confusion about where actions happen — clearer product = higher adoption from non-tech business owners.

Contacts now support a Service Date field. This allows timing review requests based on the real customer experience, not when the contact was added. Much more natural and higher response rate.

From the contact card you can now send a Quick Request instantly and see a “not contacted” tag to avoid double messaging and keep control over communication.

Finally, the new Auto Campaign is simplified: enable it, pick a delay, choose a template. Every new contact automatically receives the request after that time. No schedules, no complexity — just works.

The goal behind all of this is always the same: less explaining, more usage.
When clients understand the product without training, retention and conversions follow.

Release is planned for tomorrow, Friday 27 February.

Let us know your feedback 🙌

title: Review Gating this is important to know!
content:
We have worked in the reputation management sector for many years, and while this was previously considered a gray area, that has changed significantly.
With AI detection , especially following the most recent updates we will see many businesses, and their agencies as well, facing serious problems.
There is a reason why all major competitors have completely removed gating features. This is currently the only way to remain compliant.

Based on my research, Climbo was developed before these major changes became a reality, so there is no fault attributable to Climbo. However, as adaptation is now required from all parties, Climbo, as a reliable and trusted partner, should take a leading role.
Even Climbo could face serious consequences if prompt action is not taken.

I strongly recommend removing the gating feature entirely, in line with the approach already adopted by all major competitors.

It should not merely be optional to disable it, as the use of this feature by some parties exposes everyone to risk.

It is called the "White Label" Domino Effect

If even a small number of white-label agency resellers are identified, this could trigger a manual audit of the platform by Google’s Trust & Safety team.

[ol:1][li]The Investigation: Google looks at the source of the traffic.[li]The Link: They find that thousands of gated reviews are coming from the Climbo infrastructure.[li]The Ban: They don't just ban the specific businesses, they blacklist the entire platform.

Here are some risks for you as a agency:

FTC Enforcement: The FTC has made it clear that "deceptive" review practices are a major priority. They don't just go after the local plumber; they go after the platform or agency providing the "deceptive" software. You could face massive fines for "unfair or deceptive acts."

Client Liability \(The "Blame Game"\): If a client’s Google Business Profile gets suspended because of a strategy you recommended, you are looking at a potential breach of contract or professional negligence claim. Losing a client's entire digital presence is a great way to get sued.

title: Prospecting Tool
content:
Last week, I attended the Horecava every day, the largest hospitality industry event in the Netherlands. All major and minor horeca entrepreneurs and companies that sell products or services in this sector come together here. A few days beforehand, a list of all participating companies was made available. This was a great opportunity for me to review it using the prospecting tool.

For the companies where I saw potential opportunities, I printed out the reports and handed them over. Despite the entrepreneurs being very busy, they made time for me and appreciated that I had done my "homework." I increasingly noticed which approach resonated best with each entrepreneur. I’ve already received many commitments and a lot of interest.

This coming week, I will follow up on these leads and try to convert them into new clients. I also noticed that large companies tend to leave relatively few reviews for their business. During the event, topics such as trust, Google ranking, SEO, GEO, AI, LLM ranking, and others were discussed. At the end of the event, we toasted to celebrate the successful days.

The prospecting tool is an incredibly valuable resource! Hopefully, in the future, it will be even more beautifully designed, with more information and statistics included, but I have full confidence in that. To everyone in this new community, I would say: make good use of it!

A quick shoutout to the Climbo team. I’ve been with you for a while now, and you’ve taken the right direction with Climbo 2.0. Keep up the great work!

title: Treat Free Trial Users Like Real Clients
content:
Hello Climbers,

I want to underline something extremely important that we teach inside the lessons, and that Wayne also reinforced during his sales training.

[@Marius Georgescu](obj://user/a168e9d9dd6245cabf8647c30301d976) wrote something very powerful:

He focuses heavily on the trial experience. He doesn’t push to close immediately. He helps them collect reviews, see them published, understand the impact on visibility and trust. He talks to them personally. And when they see real results, the upgrade becomes logical.

This is exactly the mindset.

A free trial user is not a “maybe client.”
A free trial user is a future paying client — if you treat them like one.

If during the trial you:

install the review collection process properly

help them generate real reviews

show them how reviews improve their positioning

stay close and guide them to the “aha moment”

… then the upgrade is not a sales pitch.

It’s the natural next step.

The trial is not a waiting period.
It’s a proof period.

When someone experiences value, sees results, and feels supported, price becomes secondary.

So treat every trial as if they already pay you.

Keep building. 🚀

So every few weeks for at least the last 7 years i have gone to the same Pizza place, always order the same deal, always chatty with the guys there and before Christmas i was in there and i introduced myself to the owner \(he is very rarely there\) and we had a good chat nothing business just friendly conversation.

How much will this cost? I was gobsmacked, i grabbed a menu and asked him how much this advertising costs him \(50,000 printed and delivered nearly £1k\) I was amazed, at what he said next...

title: Writing reviews with AI: a good idea or not? Share your thoughts.
content:
Hi,
I'd like to know your opinion on the upcoming feature: AI-powered review generation for customers who submit a review. Here's my take on it.

First of all, I love this app and I have immense respect and gratitude for the tech team who provide all these fantastic updates, making the app better every day. However, reducing the mental load on people who don't know what to write might seem like a good idea, but it does have some drawbacks.

1\) The authenticity of a review: Here's what I think: whether positive or negative, it's what allows us to evaluate the services or purchases offered. AI will never be able to accurately describe someone's experience at a restaurant, while renting a property, or buying a product online because it can't put itself in the customer's shoes. Even if the review seems acceptable, it will inevitably lack authenticity. Personally, I wouldn't want to receive AI-generated reviews, even if they're all positive, because they don't accurately describe the customer's experience. So I think it could significantly reduce our conversion rate. I've never used AI to generate reviews for this reason, even though I use it for a lot of other things.

2\) End users might be reluctant to leave a review they didn't write and feel manipulated. So it could have the opposite effect; B2C users are much less comfortable with AI, or even resistant to its use, depending on their age group.

3\) AI analysis within the application is an excellent idea and works wonderfully. But if the reviews aren't descriptive enough, they'll come across as superficial. That's not how our customers feel.

4\) From a list of 67 competitors managing reputation or reviews that I found during my research. 45 have AI and primarily use it to respond to reviews, but what's most surprising is that none of them have a function to generate reviews from the end customer. One might think this is a good thing, but it's no coincidence…

5\) But most importantly, after doing some research online and reading the answers I found, Google states: “We don’t allow reviews primarily generated by an automated program or artificial intelligence application.” Google prohibits content “not based on real experiences” and reviews that are instigated or manipulated, and uses AI to detect fraudulent review patterns. If reviews are too generic and don’t provide sufficient description, Google might deduce that they are spam.

title: Location limit and free plan
content:
For various reasons, I don't use Stripe but my own payment system so in Climbo, I only use free plans.

Currently, the "Location limits" is set by plan, not by customer.
If you create a plan limited to 5 locations:
[ul][li]with Stripe, when the client wants to add a new location, it is redirected to Stripe and need to purchase the new location => all good[li]with the free plan, when the client wants to add a new location, it can add as many as he wants until he reaches the limit of the plan => not the behaviour I want...

I would like that when a client clicks on "Add location", it was redirected to a custom URL where he can purchase a new location and through the API I would add this new location to this customer. I can already add a location to a business through the API, I just need to prevent the client from being able to add locations by himself from the app.

In order to charge by location with free plans, the only workaround I found is to create copies of the same plan:
[ul][li]silver plan with 1 location[li]silver plan with 2 locations[li]silver plan with 3 locations[li]etc.
and change my client from 1 plan to the other when he wants to add a location.
That's much more tedious to manage and the clients needs to contact me outside the application to add a location.

[@Giacomo Chinellato](obj://user/edfd679f109c4ef4b46645bd3d030dcf) I think that would be an easy bugfix as it is just about changing the "add location" button for free plans.

title: 🛠 This Week Development Plan
content:
Hi Climbers 👋

This week we’re focusing heavily on one main thing: making review collection simpler.

One of the biggest additions will be AI Review Suggestions. The goal is simple: remove the “I don’t know what to write” friction customers often feel before leaving a review. The system will generate a ready-to-copy review based on simple rating inputs. The end customer fills a set of custom evaluation criteria and rates them from 1 to 5, with an optional note for extra context. The AI uses these ratings and notes to craft a natural-sounding review text. The business can also define specific keywords they want the AI to include in the generated review. This helps strengthen local SEO while keeping the review authentic and readable. The generated review is then simply copied by the customer and pasted wherever they prefer — Google, Facebook, Trustpilot, etc. — keeping the process flexible and platform-agnostic while removing friction for the customer.

We’re also restructuring an area that many users found confusing: the difference between Reviews and Requests. From now on, Reviews will only be the place where clients read and reply to reviews. Every action related to sending review requests will live inside Requests. Whether it’s a campaign to a list or a quick request to a single contact, the flow becomes identical: choose a template and send. No editing text mid-process, no multiple entry points — just one clear place to ask for reviews.

Auto-campaigns are getting simplified as well. The current “time + frequency” logic is not intuitive for many business owners. The new model is event-based: you enable the auto campaign, choose a delay \(for example 10 minutes\), select the template, and every new contact automatically receives the request after that delay. Easy to understand, easy to explain to clients.

To make timing even smarter, we’re introducing a Service Date field on contacts. You’ll be able to base automations either on when the contact was added or when the service actually happened — meaning you can send requests like “2 days after the appointment”, which is much more natural and improves response rates.

title: Handling the “Direct Google Reviews” Objection
content:
I understand that we can’t filter a review if customers go straight through Google. How do you usually handle this objection? I actually get this objection most of the time.

I usually respond by saying that someone who is truly upset will always find a way to leave a negative review — we can’t \(and shouldn’t\) censor people. However, in a business that genuinely delivers a good service, those cases are typically a small minority.

In any case, having a higher volume of positive reviews — whether they come through our software or not — helps push occasional negative reviews into the background.

On the other hand, if there are many unhappy customers, then it’s fair that the business receives many negative reviews. In that situation, it’s right that there isn’t a way to avoid them — and it might even mean that we wouldn’t want to work with that business in the first place.

title: Is the Reputation Report Too Problem-Oriented? A Constructive Suggestion
content:
I would like to share a reflection regarding the Reputation Analysis tool, starting with a very clear premise: the work done by the Climbo team is truly impressive.
The tool is powerful, well structured, and has enormous potential as both a commercial and consultative asset. Precisely because I strongly believe in the value of this instrument, I feel it is important to highlight a point that, in my real-world usage, has generated some concerns.
I recently used the report with a potential client who had:
[ul][li]4.7 average rating[li]170+ total reviews[li]A highly positive distribution
Despite this strong overall profile, the report placed significant emphasis on the few negative reviews present, providing corrective recommendations for issues that were statistically minimal and not recurrent.
The outcome?
The client perceived the report as “built to find a problem,” even when no structurally significant issue actually existed.
This can create two unintended effects:
[ol:1][li]Reduced credibility of the tool[li]A perception of an overly sales-driven approach
Today’s market is extremely sensitive to these nuances. For a reputational report to be truly authoritative, it should clearly distinguish between:
[ul][li]Statistical noise[li]Recurring patterns[li]Structural critical issues
In other words, the tone of the report should adapt to objective data.
If a business holds a 4.7 rating with 170+ reviews and only a very small percentage of non-recurring negative feedback, the narrative should focus on leveraging and scaling reputation, not on correcting presumed problems.
For this reason, I would like to ask Giacomo and the Climbo team whether it would be possible to allow operators the option to customize or adapt the prompt behind the report.
Even introducing:
[ul][li]Statistical significance thresholds[li]Conditional tone logic[li]The ability to choose between a “certification-oriented” or “corrective” analytical approach
I believe this would make the tool even more powerful — and above all, more credible.
